summary_text: | Option | Context-pressure relief | Operational risk (5 = lower risk) | Auditability (5 = stronger) | Rollout cost (5 = lower cost) | Verdict | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---| | Reject | 1 | 5 | 5 | 5 | Safe default, no benefit | | Narrow sidecar | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | Best tradeoff | | Deep integration | 5 | 1 | 2 | 1 | Only if Hermes becomes read-only, provenance-first, and heavily tested | My read: **narrow sidecar is the optimal choice** for FUGUE/Kernel if the goal is to relieve persistent context pressure without breaking the existing truth sources (`bootstrap receipt`, `runtime ledger`, `compact artifact`, `runtime health`). It gives real retrieval value while keeping Hermes out of authority-write paths. Deep integration is only worth considering if you can prove all of these: read-only retrieval, strict TTL/provenance, canonical-state always wins on conflict, and regression coverage across gate/health/recovery. Otherwise it adds more failure surface than it removes context pressure.
